title: Auric Cascade Systems
preview: Mid-market product analytics team. Trialled Hosted API; moving to Dedicated sizing. Needs predictable committed pricing; throughput target ~20 steady, peaks to 85, bursts 220. Security + residency requirements. Next: pricing call + final capacity worksheet.
text: Mid-market product analytics team. Trialled Hosted API; moving to Dedicated sizing. Needs predictable committed pricing; throughput target ~20 steady, peaks to 85, bursts 220. Security + residency requirements. Next: pricing call + final capacity worksheet.

title: ClearFathom AI
preview:
Inbound from web signup - small fintech startup building a customer-facing chat summarizer for transaction histories. Self-serve trial started 2025-11-03; hit token spend concerns during early tests. Main ask: practical ways to reduce hosted API spend without sacrificing answer quality. Short bullets from discovery call:
- PD (product lead) quote: "We can’t absorb the token burn if every convo is 800 tokens". looking to get to ~150 tokens avg per convo or justify cost.
- Current approach: naive full-context prompts + embeddings fallback; experimented with 2-shot examples and long system prompts.
- Wants specific recommendations: batching strategies (combine small internal requests), prefix/KV caching for repeated account queries, prompt compression (slotting user metadata vs full transcripts), and a token usage audit of current traffic.
- Security: SOC2 in roadmap; currently cloud-only, prefers Redwood hosted API (no VPC) for speed. Concerned about log retention — asked how to configure redaction/export.
- Pricing sensitivity: SMB budget, likely monthly pay-as-you-go but may commit to a small monthly reserved bundle if platform shows consistent 30-40% cost reduction via batching/caching.
- Quote from CTO on call: "If you can show me realistic per-user monthly cost with optimization steps, we can convert from trial."
- Happy path: run a 2-week token audit + implement caching hint and prompt template tweaks, measure token delta; if >=30% improvement, convert to paid monthly tier.
- Wants example prompt templates, recommended batching thresholds, and an estimate of engineering effort to add prefix caching (client vs server).
- References attached: POC prompt set (drive link), current aggregated token dump request (awaiting from them).
- Sales note: keep communications short, technical-first; hand-off to SE for a 45-min optimization workshop.

title: InkBloom Interactive
preview:
Inbound self-serve sign-up via hosted API trial. SMB web agency building marketing assistants that call internal tools (pricing lookup, ticket creation). 

Lead source and context:
- signed up with company email 2025-11-02, used free credits to run quick prototypes
- submitted support thread reporting streaming / function-calling issues 2025-11-06

Summary of current problem (customer reporting):
- Using streaming responses with structured JSON mode + functions (tool-calling). Expected: continuous chunked JSON building then final function call. Actual: intermittent malformed JSON chunks causing client-side JSON.parse failures.
- Example error reported: "JSONParseError: Unexpected token '}' in stream chunk" and "InvalidFunctionArgs: parameter 'price' expected number but got string".
- Customer paraphrase in call: "The stream looks right in the console but our frontend chokes when a chunk arrives with a partial object — we end up with trailing commas / missing braces."

What we've checked so far (timeline):
- 2025-11-03: customer built minimal prompt and function schema, hit early parse errors while using dev chrome client.
- 2025-11-04: email thread with dev sample (attached curl + schema). Gmail thread id: 1782abcd.
- 2025-11-06: SE Ethan replicated parse error in Redwood hosted sandbox using customer's schema; confirmed mismatch on numeric vs string types in schema for price and quantity. Fireflies call id for triage: ff_20251106_inkbloom.
- 2025-11-07: Support engineer created internal ticket to capture streaming chunk order and newline separator behavior (JIRA: RBW-1932).
- 2025-11-08: Quick fix suggested — normalize types on the client (coerce strings to numbers) and add server-side streaming buffer until full JSON object received. Customer tried but wants robust server-side solution.
- 2025-11-10: 30m discovery call with AE + SE + devs. Agreed next step: Redwood SE to produce a reproducible curl + minimal model call that demonstrates the malformed-chunk scenario and a recommended server-side reassembly pattern.

Technical details / notes for SE handoff (copied from customer samples):
- Function schema excerpt provided by customer (they used JSON Schema-like structure): {
  "name": "create_ticket",
  "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"title": {"type": "string"}, "price": {"type": "number"} } }
}
- Customer payloads sometimes send price as "12.99" (string) from their ecommerce tool — leads to InvalidFunctionArgs.
- Streaming behaviour: they are using client-side incremental JSON.parse per-chunk. When the model yields escaped newlines or when a chunk boundary splits a unicode escape, parse fails.
- They attempted to set "json_mode": "strict" (their term) but unclear which SDK flag they used; need to map to Redwood streaming structured output flags and example.

Customer constraints / priorities:
- Wants low-friction fix (prefer server-side guidance vs heavy client changes).
- Cost conscious: initial ARR target small; prefers conservative token budgets during repro.
- Timebox: wants working demo by end of Nov to include in client pilot.

Suggested action items (internal):
- SE (Ethan) to craft minimal repro (curl + small prompt + functions) that reproduces chunked-malformed JSON on hosted_api streaming mode. Include both correct and incorrect examples.
- Provide recommended client pattern: buffer/concatenate chunks until a full JSON object can be parsed, use content-length-like heuristics or explicit chunk delimiters when possible. Provide code snippet for Node: stream collector + try/except JSON.parse.
- Create short KB note for SMB self-serve: common structured-output pitfalls (type coercion, chunk boundary parsing, stop sequences that truncate trailing braces). Link from support.
- Confirm whether Redwood server can optionally enforce type coercion or emit canonical types in function arguments when model generates e.g., "12.99" -> 12.99. If not, document requirement clearly.

Customer quotes (verbatim-ish):
- "We love the responsiveness of the hosted API but the stream parsing is the only blocker for production."
- "If you can give a one-page snippet for our frontend team we can test in the morning."

Linked artefacts:
- Fireflies id: ff_20251106_inkbloom
- Gmail thread id: 1782abcd
- Drive doc (pricing + trial notes): https://drive.redwood/InkBloom/onboarding-pricing-v1 (customer shared view-only)
- Jira: RBW-1932 (internal support ticket capturing raw stream logs)

Next meeting: schedule 30m debug session week of 2025-11-17 (tentative).

title: Galeb Edge Systems
preview:
POC summary / deal notes:
- Customer: Galeb builds low-latency interactive features for mobile and console games. Primary product: in-game assistant + moderator + reranker for chat.
- Target SLA: p95 streaming chunk delivery <= 120ms at target concurrency (30 RPS per shard). End-to-end commit target 100-140ms.
- POC setup: 2-week realistic load run using sample game traffic, deployed on Redwood dedicated A40-class nodes behind a VPC.
- Observations: average latency nominal (~80-110ms) but p95 spiked to 350-900ms during short bursts. Streaming clients experienced visible frame drops and concatenated chunks. KV cache warm-up caused several 600-800ms stalls.
- Engineering feedback (quote): \"We saw consistent hiccups under realistic session bursts. Streaming chunk timing jitter is the showstopper for us.\"  senior platform eng.
- Mitigations attempted: tuned batch thresholds, reduced batching window, experimented with smaller logits beam size, tried a quantized 4-bit variant, increased pre-warm cache population. Improvements to median but p95 remained > target.
- Security: SSO/SAML, KMS, audit logs required; Redwood met checklists, not a blocker.
- Commercial: initial buying intent strong; requested Dedicated pricing + SLA. After two failed SLO demos and company-wide vendor consolidation, procurement placed freeze.
- Competitive note: AeroInference delivered a live demo claiming p95 <110ms using elastic model sharding; procurement favored single-vendor consolidation post-org change.
- Outreach history / timeline:
  * 2025-05-05: initial discovery call (AE Ethan).
  * 2025-05-20: technical deep-dive w/ SE (Nadia) and infra team.
  * 2025-06-01 to 2025-06-15: POC load runs.
  * 2025-06-20: POC review — p95 issues noted.
  * 2025-06-27: follow-up tuning session (INF-2173 created).
  * 2025-07-02: final demo; procurement paused budget, no reply to follow-ups.
- Current state: unresponsive since 2025-07-02. Likely closed-lost unless we demonstrate p95 improvement on a live trial.
- Recommended next steps for Redwood: capture full kernel trace and KV cache timeline, produce one-page POC root-cause summary, propose 7-day focused trial on a regional low-latency cluster with adjusted cache warm-up and streaming buffer settings. Offer discounted short-term pilot to re-open conversation.
- AE note: \"They love our routing and observability but streaming jitter killed the win. Keep on cadence; re-engage after infra fixes or new benchmark with <120ms p95.\"

title: Grayrock AI Systems
preview:
POC initiated Jan 2025 with clear scope: ingest vibration + telemetry, run embeddings + anomaly detection, prototype real-time alerting. Kickoff 2025-01-12 (AE/Ethan, SE/Priya, Head of ML/Dr. Alvarez). Data access was the first blocker — legal + infra require VPC peering and HSM/KMS for keys. Security raised requirement for ISO27001 evidence and audit logging; asked for SOC2 plus architecture diagram (uploaded to Drive).

POC plan: 4 sprints (data ingest, model onboarding, latency tuning, demo). Milestones: sprint 1 completed (data schema verified), sprint 2 missed (no engineering cycles allocated for model conversion and KV cache integration).

Timeline + activity log (high level):
- 2025-01-12: Kickoff, agreed on POC success metrics (95th pct latency < 150ms for inference; 50k events/day ingest).
- 2025-01-20: Data access request filed (infra ticket RDW-1189).
- 2025-02-05: SE working session; found third-party telemetry vendor requires contract amendment — legal escalated.
- 2025-02-18: Security review call (Fireflies FF-20250221-9b2a) — backlog: KMS integration, retention controls, residency.
- 2025-02-25: Internal reorg at Grayrock; ML lead reassigned, momentum lost.
- 2025-03-01: AE follow-up — no response from Head of ML, COO flagged budget freeze through Q2.

Customer sentiment: frustrated but not hostile. Quote from Head of Infra: "We like the performance numbers you showed but can't pull cycles until legal clears the vendor terms." Head of ML said in initial demo: "If we can get latency < 150ms at 50k events/day, this buys us a lot."

Why POC stalled: combination of internal resource shift (ML lead reassigned), contract/legal gating with telemetry vendor, and a corporate hiring freeze impacting engineering bandwidth. Security added additional controls (HSM/KMS + iso27001 evidence) that extend procurement timeline. There is no clear exec champion after reorg.

Competitive pressure: Grayrock is evaluating a managed offering from CompetitorX (claims simpler contract path) and running an internal prototype. Sales notes: risk of closed-lost if we do not re-engage exec sponsor and provide a one-page security summary + a short sandbox that requires zero infra changes.

Open action items:
- Provide one-page security architecture + SOC2 excerpt (Priya to prepare) by 2025-03-10.
- Offer a Hosted sandbox with synthetic telemetry for immediate demo (AE/Ethan proposed) — low-friction alternative to VPC.
- Request 15m sync with new Head of ML to re-establish timelines (target week of 2025-03-17).
- Prep a simple TCO comparison vs CompetitorX for procurement.

Next steps (short): reconnect, secure exec sponsor, present hosted sandbox option to avoid legal/vendor gating. If no response by 2025-03-21 mark as stalled and move to churn-retention playbook.

title: Birchwood Cascade AI
preview: Qualified via webinar. Use-case: reduce manual claims triage and surface supporting documents for adjusters. Preference for VPC/private deployment due to PHI/PII. Security asked detailed KMS and retention flow; SOC2 evidence requested. After initial intro call (1/20) engagement looked promising — N. Rao and J. Patel active. Sent pricing deck + security FAQ (links above). Repeated outreach: 1/28 (security follow-up), 2/05 (demo ask), 2/12 (LinkedIn), 2/18 (POC checklist) -> no substantive replies. Procurement message 2/22 indicated a company-wide freeze on external vendors until May; phrased as "pausing vendor projects" — unclear if permanent. No internal champion surfaced after initial call; Head of ML Ops reading messages but not committing to next steps. SE believes technical fit is strong; blocker now primarily procurement + no champion. Suggested path: final outreach (phone + LinkedIn + targeted note to CFO/procurement) to attempt soft commit for May re-eval, otherwise move to nurture track. Quote from call: "We love the latency numbers, but compliance has the final say". Next recommended milestone: confirm procurement timeline and identify sponsor by 2025-03-31. If no reply, classify as closed-nurture and revisit 2025-05.

If no reply, classify as closed-nurture and revisit 2025-05.

title: CinderWorks AI
preview:
Deal summary: Stalled / closed-lost due to unit economics. CinderWorks evaluated Redwood Dedicated + Private options for a fintech risk-screening stack. Primary objection = total run-rate (GPU + token) and size/duration of commit required to reach the quoted unit price. Selected competitor in late-Feb (ModelServeCo) citing lower committed GPU rates and shorter term trial.

Timeline & activity (shorthand):
- 2025-01-19: Intro call (AE Ethan Park, SE Rina Desai). Captured high-level requirements: 200 qps ingest, 150ms P95 latency, sensitive PII, EU data residency for a subset. (Gmail: gt_20250119_3312)
- 2025-01-28: Security kickoff w/ infra + sec. Request for KMS/HSM and SOC2 evidence. Fireflies ff_20250128_4421.
- 2025-02-03: Pricing review — provided Dedicated sizing options (4xA100 & 8xA100 sim) and Private deployment estimates. Finance asked for 12-month commitment scenarios + monthly run-rate.
- 2025-02-10: POC review call. Shared POC results: 10M docs indexed, average token per request ~420, throughput target met on dedicated pool but cost per active session > threshold. Fireflies ff_20250210_7892; POC doc drive://deals/CinderWorks/POC-results.pdf.
- 2025-02-14: Last AE follow-up; CFO pushed back on commit size. Gmail thread gt_20250214_4438.
- 2025-02-18: Internal decision to pursue ModelServeCo; deal moved to closed-lost in CRM.

Requirements / ask (from customer):
- Deployment: VPC or on-prem for regulated workloads (banking KYC, AML datasets).
- Security: SSO/SAML + SOC2 + KMS/HSM + tamper-proof audit logs + retention controls.
- Performance: 150ms tail latency, burst to 400 qps, embedding store for near-real-time search.
- Cost target: CFO asked for demonstrable blended unit cost that keeps annual run-rate < $700k (company target).

Key pain points discovered:
- Token economics: POC showed higher-than-expected tokens per session (avg 420) — bumping per-session cost. Wanted 30% lower per-token effective cost.
- GPU run-rate: Forecasted steady-state GPU hours made CFO nervous about long 12-month commit at quoted levels. Wanted shorter trial or consumption-first pricing.
- Champion turnover: Lead ML engineer (Anika Rao) resigned mid-POC; institutional knowledge loss slowed internal momentum.
- Security lead requested on-prem HSM integration that would add 6-8 weeks; customer timeline did not allow.

Quotes / notable lines:
- CTO (via call): "We need line-item run-rate visibility and can't risk a 12-month commit at this price. If we can't get that number down 30% we're out."
- CFO (email): "Nice results but economics don't pencil vs. other offers. Can you provide a staged commit with milestoned pricing?"

Outcome / reason lost: Pricing/unit economics. ModelServeCo offered a stepped-commit with deeper GPU discounts for first 6 months + reserved preemptible capacity that matched CinderWorks' short-term budget constraints. Redwood's private option required additional project scoping and timeline that pushed delivery beyond CinderWorks' go-live window.

Next steps recommended by AE/SE:
- Mark closed-lost, schedule re-engage in 6 months post infra refresh.
- Prepare a short note comparing stepped-commit options and a compact case study showing how batching + prefix caching could reduce effective token cost by ~20% (deliverable: cost-sensitivity one-pager).
- Leave security docs and POC artifacts in drive link for internal reference.

Blocking list for re-open: unit_economics; need smaller initial commit or consumption-first pilot; rehire of ML champion; faster HSM/HSM-integration path.

Related artifacts: drive://deals/CinderWorks/cost-model-v2.xlsx, drive://deals/CinderWorks/security-answers.pdf, SUP-8217 (ticket for KMS integration question).

Notes style: CRM bullets, shorthand, call quotes.

- 2025-02-10: POC review call. Shared POC results: 10M docs indexed, average token per request ~420, throughput target met on dedicated pool but cost per active session > threshold. Fireflies ff_20250210_7892; POC doc drive://deals/CinderWorks/POC-results.pdf.
- 2025-02-14: Last AE follow-up; CFO pushed back on commit size. Gmail thread gt_20250214_4438.
- 2025-02-18: Internal decision to pursue ModelServeCo; deal moved to closed-lost in CRM.

Requirements / ask (from customer):
- Deployment: VPC or on-prem for regulated workloads (banking KYC, AML datasets).
- Security: SSO/SAML + SOC2 + KMS/HSM + tamper-proof audit logs + retention controls.
- Performance: 150ms tail latency, burst to 400 qps, embedding store for near-real-time search.
- Cost target: CFO asked for demonstrable blended unit cost that keeps annual run-rate < $700k (company target).

Key pain points discovered:
- Token economics: POC showed higher-than-expected tokens per session (avg 420) — bumping per-session cost. Wanted 30% lower per-token effective cost.
- GPU run-rate: Forecasted steady-state GPU hours made CFO nervous about long 12-month commit at quoted levels. Wanted shorter trial or consumption-first pricing.
- Champion turnover: Lead ML engineer (Anika Rao) resigned mid-POC; institutional knowledge loss slowed internal momentum.
- Security lead requested on-prem HSM integration that would add 6-8 weeks; customer timeline did not allow.

Quotes / notable lines:
- CTO (via call): "We need line-item run-rate visibility and can't risk a 12-month commit at this price. If we can't get that number down 30% we're out."
- CFO (email): "Nice results but economics don't pencil vs. other offers. Can you provide a staged commit with milestoned pricing?"

Outcome / reason lost: Pricing/unit economics. ModelServeCo offered a stepped-commit with deeper GPU discounts for first 6 months + reserved preemptible capacity that matched CinderWorks' short-term budget constraints. Redwood's private option required additional project scoping and timeline that pushed delivery beyond CinderWorks' go-live window.

Next steps recommended by AE/SE:
- Mark closed-lost, schedule re-engage in 6 months post infra refresh.
- Prepare a short note comparing stepped-commit options and a compact case study showing how batching + prefix caching could reduce effective token cost by ~20% (deliverable: cost-sensitivity one-pager).
- Leave security docs and POC artifacts in drive link for internal reference.

Blocking list for re-open: unit_economics; need smaller initial commit or consumption-first pilot; rehire of ML champion; faster HSM/HSM-integration path.

Related artifacts: drive://deals/CinderWorks/cost-model-v2.xlsx, drive://deals/CinderWorks/security-answers.pdf, SUP-8217 (ticket for KMS integration question).

Notes style: CRM bullets, shorthand, call quotes.

title: Fountwell Research
preview:
POC summary: building a RAG-powered knowledge portal for sell-side research and internal desk research. Primary pipeline: nightly ingestion of PDFs/earnings_transcripts + SEC filings -> paragraph-level passages -> embeddings store -> retrieve+rerank. Index size target: ~120M passages (50-70M docs collapsed to passages). Latency/throughput needs: interactive search for analysts (p95 < 120ms on rerank path), bursty workloads during open/close with sustained 400-600 QPS and peaks to 1k QPS. Cost sensitivity: procurement asked for unit economics — target $0.005-0.02 per query depending on one vs two-stage rerank. Security/residency: data must stay in NA region, customer-managed KMS keys required, SOC2 + audit logging mandatory. Preferred deployment: Dedicated pools in VPC; evaluating private/air-gapped on prem for regulated clients.

Key quotes: "We can't accept any residency leakage — keys must be customer-controlled." — Head of Data (K. Morales). "If p95 can't hit ~120ms for our 2-stage rerank, we need to consider simpler rerank or more capacity." — CTO.

POC approach discussed: 1) 10M sample index run on hosted API to validate similarity metrics (done), 2) 50M staging index on Dedicated (requested sizing), 3) full-scale 120M run for latency/cost verification. POC metrics to capture: embedding QPS, vector search latency, CPU/GPU utilization on Dedicated nodes, cost per query breakdown (embedding + ANN + rerank), index rebuild time, KV/cache hit rates.

Legal/Procurement notes: DPA + KMS SOW outstanding. Internal procurement requests line-item for reserved capacity (12-36 months) and breach SLA credits. Security requested HSM integration details and key rotation policy.

Sales/next touch: SE to deliver Dedicated sizing worksheet and cost-model by 2025-02-20. AE to schedule final security review w/ security engineer and legal on 2025-02-25. Procurement kick-off planned after SOW sign-off.

title: Archipelago Enterprise Solutions
preview: POC for private VPC deployment. Must integrate with customer KMS, deliver audit logs to Splunk, enforce SSO via SAML, meet US-only data residency for PHI-adjacent datasets. Low tolerance for fallbacks to public endpoints. Prefer quantized open models for cost control. Need canary rollback and A/B routing during rollout.

title: Cascade Fintech Solutions

title: Auriga FinServ Intelligence Hub
preview:
Key account for Q1 push — R&D + Investment Research teams consolidated 3 legacy indices. Preference for dedicated capacity with private control plane. Primary ask: scale to ~30M docs (mixed PDFs, time-series tables) with sub-400ms 95th-percentile rerank latency. Cost sensitivity: goal ~<$0.002/query for vector + rerank on average workload.

Meeting snippets:
- "We need deterministic latency across European trading hours — spikes during market open are non-negotiable."
- Engineer: "want to avoid per-request cold starts; prefix/KV cache critical".

They are evaluating Redwood for ability to: (1) host embeddings at scale with incremental indexing; (2) run a dense-retrieval + cross-encoder rerank pipeline; (3) integrate KMS/HSM and meet EU residency + retention policy. CSM call 2025-01-20 confirmed can move to procurement if SOW matches latency SLA and KMS certs.

title: Coppergate Inference Works
preview: Workload: synchronous per-request reranking for auction pipeline (95ms target p95), background embeddings for offline features.,Throughput target: sustained 120k tokens/sec across cluster during business hours, ability to scale to 350k tokens/sec for 60-second bursts during peak auctions.,Concurrency caps: prefer soft concurrency limit per-tenant (200 concurrent requests) with queueing for short spikes; hard limit for safety at 600 concurrent requests.,Burst policy: allow burst beyond reserved tokens/sec for up to 90s, billed separately or charged against burst pool; customer requests burst not to cannibalize reserved throughput metrics used for SLA credits.,Latency SLOs: p50 < 40ms, p95 < 95ms, p99 < 200ms for the reranking path when under reserved capacity.,Model prefs: 7B-13B dense models for latency budget; interested in quantized variants if Redwood can guarantee p95 latency at target throughput.,Routing/fallback: automatic fallback to smaller model family or dedicated low-cost pool if reserved capacity saturated; require predictable downgrade behavior and traceability in Console.,Security: SSO (SAML) integration required, customer-managed KMS for keying, SOC2 compliance evidence and audit log retention for 365 days.,Measurement: want tokens/sec measured at request ingress and also validated by Redwood's console (token accounting) with agreed aggregation window (5-minute and 1-hour rolling averages).

title: GlacierBird Creative Suite
preview:
Overview: GlacierBird is an SMB marketing/content platform powering automated social & email campaigns for ~150 small brand customers. High-volume templated generation (social posts, short-form copy, email variants). They estimate 200k-800k generated items/month once scaled; unit cost matters. Interested in Redwood Hosted API for self-serve onboarding + Optimize suggestions for batching/caching recommendations.

Recent timeline:
- 2024-11-05: Inbound form -> signed up for quickstart. Assigned AE (Maya).
- 2024-11-08: Intro email + requested price sheet for high-volume tiers (see Drive pricing deck).
- 2024-11-15: 40-min discovery call (recorded, ff_2024-11-15_glacierbird_intro). Sec/ops lead + 2 devs attended. Discussed typical throughput and retry behavior.
- 2024-11-18: Dev follow-up asked for real token-cost examples for batching 20->200 requests and prefix caching options.
- 2024-11-20: Sent initial throughput targets and asked for sample POC keys.

Requirements / Signals:
- Workload type: batch content generation (templated), social_posting, email_personalization, light embeddings for dedupe/duplicate detection.
- Latency: per-request < 1s is nice-to-have for interactive authoring; bulk jobs can be higher.
- Throughput: bursts up to 1k reqs/min during nightly campaign generation.
- Cost sensitivity: primary constraint; looking to cut per-item cost via batching + prefix caching.
- Model prefs: open to smaller open models (quantized) for drafts, fallback to higher-quality model for final variants; want price/quality tradeoff guidance.
- Routing/fallback: interested in policy that falls back to cheaper variant under load.
- Security: SSO for admin console, audit logs for team activity; SOC2 optional but helpful. Data residency not critical (US customers).

Quotes / Call snippets:
- "We need predictable per-email costs — if it spikes we kill campaigns."
- "Batching + caching look like the quickest win. Give us numbers."

Linked artifacts:
- Pricing deck: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1GlacierBirdPricingDeck
- Security FAQ stub: https://drive.google.com/file/d/GlacierBird_Security_FAQ
- Internal thread with devs: thread-CA1234glacierbird

Next steps (short):
- Send detailed 1M-token cost scenarios (3 variants: naive, batched, cached).
- Provide a 2-week POC plan + temporary hosted keys for devs to test batching and prefix caching (requested).
- Quick follow-up demo week of 11/27 focusing on Optimize suggestions and how to measure per-item economics.

Blockers (short):
- Need clearer benchmarks showing token/unit cost with batching + caching.
- Legal wants a short note on data retention & audit logging before trial keys.

AE notes: Good inbound — marketing teams love UI and templates. Prioritize cost examples and a simple POC flow. Keep messaging concrete ("$ / 1k emails with X batching") and include fallback routing options.

title: Foxglove Insightworks
preview:
Account background:
- SMB -> mid-market SaaS (productivity suite for distributed teams). Heavy search + chat in product. Interested in moving inference off self-host (cost spikes) to managed + dedicated for predictable throughput.

Timeline / recent activity:
- 2024-06-12: Intro call (AE Ethan) - high-level product fit, baseline usage ~15M tokens/mo, heavy daytime burst traffic.
- 2024-06-25: Hosted API demo (Priya) - showed chat streaming, function calling. Customer liked latency and streaming UX.
- 2024-07-05: Security questionnaire sent; FO sent SOC2 docs.
- 2024-07-12: Deep-arch call (Fireflies ff_20240712_0421) - discussed Dedicated architecture, VPC peering, KMS.
- 2024-07-24: POC kickoff (internal infra + Redwood SE). Meeting notes: agreed POC scope, success metrics (see below).
- 2024-07-30: Canary config review; finance asked for cost simulation.

Use-case & requirements (customer stated):
- Primary: in-app chat assistant (multi-turn), secondaries: embeddings for semantic search, reranking for content recommendations.
- Latency SLOs: p50 < 120ms, p95 < 400ms for short-chat (<=256 tokens). Throughput: 600 reqs/min steady, spikes to 2.5k/min in morning window.
- Cost sensitivity: wants predictable commit for Dedicated. Target unit cost to be <= current self-hosted monthly spend.
- Model preferences: open-models preferred (Llama 2 / Mistral-family variants) but willing to use Redwood-curated variants. Need model-variant routing for quality/cost trade-offs.
- Security: SSO/SAML, tenant-level audit logs, ability to use customer KMS, data residency (US only) for certain workloads.

POC for Dedicated (agreed scope):
- Duration: 4 weeks (pilot phase) + 2-week canary in production traffic slice.
- Goal: demonstrate Dedicated pool can meet p95 latency target under 80% of expected peak traffic; show graceful fallback to Hosted model variant when Dedicated overloaded.
- Canary plan: start with 2% of traffic -> 10% -> 25% -> 50% over 5 days if metric pass. Canary metrics: latencies (p50/p95), error rate (<0.5%), cost per 1k tokens.
- Observability: Redwood Console dashboards for token-level cost, per-route latency, tracing. SE will attach tracing hooks into customer front-end.

Fallback & routing strategy (detailed):
- Primary route: Dedicated high-perf variant (quantized FP16 Llama2-70B-vq) in us-west region.
- Secondary variants (fallbacks): cheaper quantized model variant (Llama2-13B-quant) for cost fallback; and Hosted endpoint (Redwood-hosted) in us-east as cross-region fallback.
- Routing policy: policy-based routing with thresholds: if Dedicated queueing latency > 150ms OR CUDA mem pressure triggers > 85% for >30s => perform gradual traffic shift to fallback variant per-request ratio (10% increments every 30s) until stabilized or rollback triggered.
- Region failover: automatic region failover to us-east if telemetry reports degraded hardware (GPU OOM / node failures) across >30% of the Dedicated pool.
- Rollback triggers: increase in error-rate >0.8% over baseline OR p95 latency > 2x target for >2 minutes. Manual rollback allowed via Redwood Console and API.
- Cache strategy: enable prefix/KV caching for repeated conversation context to reduce tokenization cost; Redwood Optimize to recommend batching size and quantization profile before POC week 2.

POC success criteria (customer + Redwood):
- Functional: chat flows show no more than 0.5% failure in edge cases, test prompt set outputs within acceptable quality thresholds (customer curated eval set).
- Performance: p95 latency <= 400ms under 80% nominal peak (measured for 3 hours sustained).
- Cost: projected monthly spend for Dedicated (with agreed commit) <= $120k/month (finance to approve).

Commercial & procurement notes:
- Early pricing discussed: 12-mo reserved capacity with monthly billing; customer requests a 30% uplift cap for overage. Priya to prepare detailed pricing (drive:/Redwood/pricing/foxglove-poc-pricing-2024-07.pdf).
- Legal/data residency: customer legal flagged need for US-only residency on PHI-adjacent metadata. SEC ticket opened (SEC-5789).

Open items / blockers (short):
- Legal needs sample contract clause for data residency + audit rights.
- Infra needs temporary GPU quota increase to run canary.
- Finance needs final cost simulation vs current self-hosted.

Quotes / snippets from stakeholders:
- Product lead: "We need predictability — spikes are killing our infra budget."
- CTO: "If we can get a clean canary with rollback and region failover, we'll be comfortable moving core chat to Dedicated."

Next steps (90d plan):
- Week 1: Redwood SE deploys Dedicated pool; create canary route and tracing integration.
- Week 2: Run synthetic load tests + Optimize recommendations (batching, qprofile).
- Week 3-4: Controlled canary with live traffic; monitor SLOs and cost.
- Week 5: Exec review + pricing sign-off -> procurement commences.

Notes author: Ethan Park (AE) + Priya Narang (SE).
